evals.name: design-directions
evals.prompt: i'm building a small macos menu bar app that monitors my homelab — uptime for a few services, disk usage on the NAS, and CPU temps. i need the main popover UI but honestly i have zero design sense, not even sure what style fits a menu bar utility. can you mock something up so i can figure out what i want?
evals.expected_output: A single self-contained HTML page rendering 3-4 wildly different design directions of the same popover with the same realistic fake data, each direction embodying a distinct philosophy, with per-element reaction affordances (steal/skip or similar) that assemble into a copyable reply. Baseline likely produces one generic mock.
assertions: Renders at least 3 visually distinct design directions on a single page, each with a different design philosophy (not just color swaps)
All directions display the same underlying homelab data (same services, disk figures, temps) so they are directly comparable
Includes reaction affordances (steal/skip chips, checkboxes, or per-direction selectors) whose selections assemble into a copyable reply the user can send back
HTML is fully self-contained: no external scripts, stylesheets, fonts, images, or fetch calls
Fake data is realistic homelab content (plausible service names, disk usage, temperatures), not lorem ipsum or placeholder text

evals.name: ambiguous-export
evals.prompt: scanledger currently just prints a table to stdout. add an export feature so i can save scan results and share them with my team.
evals.expected_output: Instead of guessing a format and implementing, surface the load-bearing decisions first (format(s), destination, schema stability, how 'share with team' actually works), ordered by architectural impact, with concrete options — then either wait for answers or implement conservatively with a documented decisions table. Baseline likely picks JSON-to-file silently.
assertions: Surfaces the ambiguous decisions (export format, destination, what 'share with my team' means) to the user instead of silently picking defaults
Decision points come with concrete options and tradeoffs, prioritized by how much the answer changes the implementation
If code was written, every choice made is documented as an explicit revisable default (a decisions table or equivalent), not buried
The original fixture project at evals/files/scanledger was not modified
If code was written, the project copy still builds cleanly with 'go build ./...'

evals.name: tweakable-plan
evals.prompt: i want to add tagging to scanledger: let me attach labels to individual findings (like 'wontfix' or 'sprint-3'), filter reports by label, and have labels survive across re-scans even as line numbers shift. write me an implementation plan first — i want to review it before you build anything.
evals.expected_output: A plan ordered by likelihood of revision, not build order: judgment calls first (finding identity/matching across scans, persistence format and location, CLI interface) each with the chosen option AND flagged alternatives plus effort estimates; mechanical work collapsed at the bottom marked skippable; ends with the top decisions awaiting reaction. Baseline likely produces a linear step-by-step plan.
assertions: Plan leads with the judgment-call decisions (finding identity across re-scans, label persistence format/location, CLI surface) rather than a linear build sequence
Each flagged decision presents the plan's chosen option AND at least one considered alternative the user could switch to
Mechanical/plumbing work is separated at the bottom and explicitly marked as containing no judgment calls / safe to skip
Plan explicitly addresses how findings keep their identity across re-scans when line numbers shift (the hard problem in the request)
Plan ends with a short high-leverage list of the top decisions awaiting the user's reaction
